section.title: Rebuilding the Altar and Foundation
section.sectionRationale: Before a single wall of the temple rises, the returned remnant rebuilds the altar and restores the sacrifices, keeps the appointed feasts, and then lays the foundation with worship. The chapter shows a people whose first instinct is to draw near to God in the way He commanded, and it ends with the strange, mingled sound of joy and weeping at what has begun.
section.thesis: The restoration of God's people begins at the altar, with obedient worship offered in fear yet in faith, so that when the foundation is laid the community responds with a mixture of soaring joy and grief that only a greater temple could finally answer.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the altar rebuilt and the sacrifices and feasts restored, verses 1 to 6; preparations and the laying of the foundation, verses 7 to 10; and the great sound of joy and weeping, verses 11 to 13.

units.label: The altar rebuilt and worship restored
units.note: as it is written in the Law of Moses

verses.text: By the seventh month, the Israelites had settled in their towns, and the people assembled as one man in Jerusalem.

Then Jeshua son of Jozadak and his fellow priests, along with Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and his associates, began to build the {{mizbeach|altar}} of the God of Israel to sacrifice burnt offerings on it, as it is written in the {{torah|Law of Moses}} the man of God.
They set up the altar on its foundation and sacrificed {{olah|burnt offerings}} on it to the LORD, both the morning and evening burnt offerings, even though they feared the people of the land.
They also celebrated the Feast of Tabernacles in accordance with what is written, and they offered burnt offerings daily based on the number prescribed for each day.
After that, they presented the regular burnt offerings and those for New Moons and for all the appointed sacred feasts of the LORD, as well as all the freewill offerings brought to the LORD.
On the first day of the seventh month, the Israelites began to offer burnt offerings to the LORD, although the foundation of the temple of the LORD had not been laid.

units.label: The foundation of the temple laid
units.note: cedar from Lebanon, and Levites to supervise

verses.text: They gave money to the masons and carpenters, and food and drink and oil to the people of Sidon and Tyre to bring cedar logs from Lebanon to Joppa by sea, as authorized by Cyrus king of Persia.

In the second month of the second year after they had arrived at the house of God in Jerusalem, Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, Jeshua son of Jozadak, and the rest of their associates including the priests, the Levites, and all who had returned to Jerusalem from the captivity, began the work. They appointed Levites twenty years of age or older to supervise the construction of the house of the LORD.

So Jeshua and his sons and brothers, Kadmiel and his sons (descendants of Yehudah), and the sons of Henadad and their sons and brothers, all Levites, joined together to supervise those working on the house of God.

units.label: Joy and weeping at the foundation
units.note: the people could not distinguish the shouts from the weeping

verses.text: When the builders had laid the foundation of the temple of the LORD, the priests in their apparel with trumpets, and the Levites (the sons of Asaph) with cymbals, took their positions to praise the LORD, as David king of Israel had prescribed.

And they sang responsively with praise and thanksgiving to the LORD: “For He is good; for His {{chesed|loving devotion}} to Israel endures forever.” Then all the people gave a great shout of praise to the LORD, because the foundation of the house of the LORD had been laid.
But many of the older priests, Levites, and family heads who had seen the first temple wept loudly when they saw the foundation of this temple. Still, many others shouted joyfully.
The people could not distinguish the shouts of joy from the sound of weeping, because the people were making so much noise. And the sound was heard from afar.

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, literally a place of sacrifice, from the verb to slaughter. The remnant builds the altar first, before the temple itself, because atoning worship is the foundation of their life with God. Every altar in Israel points forward to the cross, where the true and final sacrifice is offered.

torah.gloss: The Law, or instruction, of Moses. The people do not improvise their worship; they act as it is written. The recurring phrase in accordance with what is written shows a community determined to obey God's revealed word, the pattern the whole book commends.

olah.gloss: The burnt offering, literally that which goes up, wholly consumed on the altar and ascending as smoke to God. It expressed total dedication and atonement. Its complete surrender foreshadows Christ, who offered Himself wholly to God for us.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love, loyalty, and mercy. The refrain sung at the foundation, His loving devotion to Israel endures forever, is Israel's great confession of God's faithful love, the same love that carries His people through exile and home again, and that meets us fully in Christ.

basic.context: Once settled in their towns, the returned exiles gather in Jerusalem in the seventh month and, before building the temple, they rebuild the altar and start the sacrifices again, keeping the Feast of Tabernacles exactly as the Law commands. They do this even though they are afraid of the hostile peoples around them. Then they lay the temple's foundation with a service of praise, and the crowd erupts in a sound that is joy and weeping at once. The question is: where does true restoration begin? At the altar, with God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative history, showing us a good example rather than issuing commands. The repeated phrase as it is written signals the point: worship should follow God's word, not our preferences. We should not copy the animal sacrifices, which are fulfilled in Christ, but we should copy the priority the people gave to worshiping God rightly, promptly, and together, even under pressure.
basic.originalLanguages: The first thing rebuilt is the mizbeach, the altar, the place of sacrifice. They offer the olah, the burnt offering that goes up wholly to God, and they do it according to the torah, the Law of Moses. At the foundation they sing the great refrain of God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love that endures forever.
basic.typology: The altar and burnt offerings point to the cross, where Christ is the true sacrifice offered wholly to God. The temple foundation points to Christ the cornerstone, on whom God's true house is built.
basic.christ: The people put the altar first because they knew they could not come to God without atonement. That instinct is answered forever in Jesus, the once-for-all sacrifice, our true altar and Lamb. The mingled joy and weeping over a lesser temple looks ahead to the promise that God would fill His house with greater glory, a promise kept when Jesus, greater than the temple, walked into it.
basic.god: God is worthy of obedient, ordered worship, and He is faithful: the very refrain the people sing celebrates His covenant love that never ends. He receives the worship of a fearful, weak remnant and is pleased to dwell among them again.
basic.crossReferences: Haggai 2:3-9 addresses the disappointment over the smaller temple and promises greater glory. Hebrews 10 shows the burnt offerings fulfilled in Christ's one sacrifice. And 1 Corinthians 3:11 names Christ as the only foundation.
basic.oneStory: Restoration begins at the altar, because fellowship with God always rests on sacrifice. The remnant's obedient worship, and the tears over a temple that could not match the old, set us longing for the greater temple and the final sacrifice, both of which arrive in Jesus.

context.literary: The chapter is arranged around worship: first the altar and the feasts, then the foundation laid with a full liturgical celebration modeled on David's arrangements. It builds to the memorable image of verses 12 and 13, where praise and lament blend into one indistinguishable sound, a deliberately unresolved note that the following chapters, and ultimately the prophets, will address.
context.historical: The seventh month held the great autumn feasts, including Tabernacles, making it the fitting time to restore worship. Cedar from Lebanon shipped through Sidon and Tyre to Joppa echoes the building of Solomon's temple, tying this modest project to the glorious original. The older generation who wept had seen Solomon's temple before its destruction some fifty years earlier, so their grief was personal and real.
context.canonical: This foundation-laying is the event the prophets Haggai and Zechariah later address. Haggai 2 directly speaks to those who despised the day of small things and promises that the glory of the latter house will exceed the former, a promise pointing beyond stone to the coming of Christ, the Lord who would suddenly come to His temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the restored community rightly made obedient, God-ordained worship its first priority, rebuilding the altar and restoring the sacrifices before the temple itself, and to capture the honest mixture of joy and grief that attended a real but humble beginning.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the restoration of the Mosaic sacrificial system, which is not prescribed for the church since Christ has fulfilled it. What abides is the pattern of worshiping God according to His word, the priority of atonement in approaching Him, and the call to press on in obedience even when the work feels small and the opposition looms.

debates.question: Why did the older men weep when the foundation was laid?

views.label: Grief at a lesser glory
views.case: Those who remembered Solomon's magnificent temple saw how modest this one would be; Haggai 2:3 confirms the comparison, so their weeping expressed genuine sorrow that the restored house could not match the former glory.

views.label: Mixed sorrow and repentant memory
views.case: Their tears also recalled the sin and judgment that had destroyed the first temple; the weeping mingled loss, humility, and a chastened awareness of how far the nation had fallen, even as others rejoiced at God's mercy in beginning again.

debates.bearing: Both readings set up the prophetic answer: God would not leave His people to mourn a small beginning, but promised in Haggai 2:9 to fill the latter house with greater glory, fulfilled in the coming of Christ.

debates.question: Why rebuild the altar before the temple?

views.label: Atonement and worship come first
views.case: The people could not wait for a finished building to draw near to God; they needed the altar's sacrifices for atonement and daily fellowship at once, showing that right worship, not architecture, is the heart of restoration.

views.label: Obedience to the written pattern
views.case: The Law centered Israel's life on sacrifice at the appointed place, so restoring the altar and the feasts as it is written was the obedient way to re-establish covenant life, even before the temple structure rose.

debates.bearing: Either way, the priority is clear: God's people begin their renewal at the altar, in atoning, obedient worship, a priority answered finally at the cross.

study.originalLanguages: The remnant's first act of building is the mizbeach, the altar, the place of slaughter and sacrifice, because life with God begins with atonement. On it they offer the olah, the burnt offering, literally that which goes up, wholly consumed and ascending to God as a sign of total dedication. Everything is done according to the torah, the instruction of Moses; the narrator repeats in accordance with what is written to stress a worship shaped by God's word rather than human invention. And when the foundation is laid, the people sing Israel's oldest refrain of praise, celebrating the LORD's chesed, His steadfast covenant love and loyalty that endures forever. The words gather the whole chapter into one confession: a redeemed people, drawing near through sacrifice, resting on the unfailing love of God.

typology.type: The altar and the burnt offering
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and final sacrifice offered wholly to God, and the altar to which believers now come, Hebrews 10:11-14 and 13:10.
Christ the cornerstone and only foundation of God's true house, 1 Corinthians 3:11 and Ephesians 2:20.
God tabernacling among His people, fulfilled when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, John 1:14 and Revelation 21:3.

study.christ: The people's deepest instinct, to build the altar first, reveals a truth the gospel makes plain: no one comes to God apart from sacrifice. The morning and evening burnt offerings they restored could only point forward; they could never take away sin. Jesus is the reality they foreshadowed, the Lamb offered once for all, our altar and our atonement, so that we may draw near with confidence. And the ache of the weeping elders, mourning a house that could not rival Solomon's, is met in Christ, the greater glory of the latter temple, the Lord who came suddenly to His temple and who is Himself the meeting place of God and man.
study.god: God is worthy of careful, obedient, wholehearted worship, and the remnant honors Him by doing everything as it is written. He is faithful beyond His people's failures: the very song they raise at the foundation confesses that His loving devotion endures forever, the covenant love that pursued them into exile and carried them home. And He is gracious to receive the worship of a small, frightened, imperfect community, delighting to dwell again in their midst. The God of this chapter is holy, faithful, and near.

crossReferences.note: God addresses those who despised the smaller temple and promises that the glory of the latter house will surpass the former.
The repeated Old Testament sacrifices are fulfilled and ended by Christ's single, perfect offering.
No one can lay any foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ.
The refrain sung at the foundation, His loving devotion endures forever, echoes Israel's great song of covenant praise.

study.oneStory: True restoration starts at the altar, for fellowship with God always rests on atoning sacrifice. The remnant's obedient worship, and the tears of those who longed for a greater glory, leave the story reaching forward: to the promise of a house filled with glory, and to the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ, who is both the greater temple and the final altar. In Him the mingled weeping and joy of God's people finally resolves into pure and lasting praise.

quiz.q: What did the returned exiles build first, before the temple itself?

options.text: The altar, so they could restore the sacrifices and worship God at once.
options.note: Verses 2-3 show the altar rebuilt first, prioritizing atoning worship.

options.text: The city walls, for protection against their enemies.
options.note: The walls come much later, in Nehemiah; here the priority is the altar.

options.text: Their own houses and marketplaces.
options.note: Personal housing is not the focus here; worship of God comes first.

options.text: A palace for Zerubbabel.
options.note: No palace is built; the concern is the worship of God.

quiz.q: What phrase repeatedly describes how they carried out their worship?

options.text: As it is written, or in accordance with what is written, following the Law of Moses.
options.note: The chapter stresses obedience to God's revealed word in worship.

options.text: As seemed best to the leaders at the time.
options.note: The opposite: they deliberately followed the written pattern, not their own judgment.

options.text: As the surrounding nations worshiped their gods.
options.note: They distanced themselves from the peoples of the land, worshiping as God commanded.

options.text: In secret, so no one would notice.
options.note: Their worship was public and their praise was heard from afar.

quiz.q: Why was the sound at the laying of the foundation so confusing?

options.text: Shouts of joy and loud weeping mixed together and could not be told apart.
options.note: Verses 12-13 describe joy and grief blending into one great sound.

options.text: The trumpets drowned out the singing entirely.
options.note: The instruments were part of it, but the confusion was joy mixed with weeping.

options.text: The people were arguing about how to build.
options.note: It was worship, not argument, that filled the air.

options.text: An enemy attack interrupted the ceremony.
options.note: No attack occurs here; the mingled sound was joy and weeping.

quiz.q: Why did many of the older men weep at the foundation?

options.text: They had seen Solomon's temple and grieved that this one seemed so much smaller.
options.note: Verse 12 and Haggai 2:3 point to sorrow over the lesser glory.

options.text: They did not believe God had brought them home.
options.note: They did believe; their grief was over the diminished temple, not unbelief.

options.text: They were angry at Zerubbabel's leadership.
options.note: The text names sorrow over the former temple, not anger at leaders.

options.text: They wanted to return to Babylon.
options.note: Nothing suggests a wish to return; they wept over the smaller house.

quiz.q: How does the altar-first priority point to Christ?

options.text: It shows that fellowship with God rests on sacrifice, fulfilled once for all in Christ, our true altar and Lamb.
options.note: The sacrifices foreshadow Christ's final, atoning offering.

options.text: It teaches that we must still offer animal sacrifices today.
options.note: Christ has fulfilled and ended the sacrifices; we do not repeat them.

options.text: It proves buildings matter more than worship.
options.note: The point is the reverse: worship and atonement come before the building.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: The altar and sacrifices point directly to Christ's atoning work.

questions: The remnant made worship of God their very first priority, even before securing their own comfort. What does the order of your priorities reveal about where God sits in your life?
They worshiped as it is written, shaping their approach to God by His word rather than their own ideas. Where might you be tempted to worship God on your own terms instead of His?
They rebuilt the altar even though they feared the hostile peoples around them. Where is fear tempting you to hold back from obeying God, and what would faithful obedience look like anyway?
The same event brought some to tears and others to shouts of joy. How do you hold honest grief and real thanksgiving together in a season that is both hard and hopeful?
God promised to fill the latter temple with greater glory, fulfilled in Christ. How does knowing that your best days are secured in Jesus reshape the way you face small and disappointing beginnings?

translationNotes.note: The clause both the morning and evening burnt offerings, set off by dashes in some editions, is rendered here with commas; it specifies the twice-daily offerings prescribed in the Law.
Loving devotion renders the Hebrew chesed; the BSB footnote notes its range, love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and covenant loyalty. The refrain echoes Psalm 136 and 1 Chronicles 16:34.
Those who had seen the first temple were elderly, since some fifty years had passed since its destruction; their comparison is the concern Haggai later addresses directly.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 3:1-13. Em dashes in verses 3 and 9 were rendered as commas per house style; no em or en dashes remain.
